Subject: Auth migration complete — JWT + Google/GitHub SSO are live

Hi [Acme contact name],

Great news — our auth migration is finished and the new system is live.

What shipped
- Short-lived JWT access tokens and a refreshed auth backend (more secure, more reliable).
- OAuth2 SSO with Google and GitHub — your users can now sign in with those providers.
- Updated developer flows so your engineers can use JWTs for API access and integrations.

Why this matters for Acme
- Faster, more reliable sign-in for users who use Google/GitHub accounts.
- Cleaner, standards-based tokens for API calls and integrations.
- Fewer session-related issues and smoother developer experience when calling our APIs.

How to get started
1) Try sign-in: have a few users sign in with Google or GitHub and confirm access.
2) Integration guide & docs: [link to Auth & API docs — insert URL]
3) Need help? Alan (engineering) can run a short setup/verification session and walk your team through any changes. Josh (sales) will handle coordination and commercial questions.

A note about SAML (your IT asked about this)
- We heard your request for SAML SSO. The current release does not include SAML — it’s a different protocol and requires additional work.
- Based on our scoping, adding SAML would take roughly 3 weeks of engineering time. We did not include it in this phase to get the improved auth system out sooner.
- If SAML is a hard requirement for your rollout, we can prioritize it. Please let us know whether you want us to:
  a) Start a SAML implementation now (we’ll provide a firm timeline and a prioritized plan), or
  b) Use OAuth2 SSO immediately while we coordinate a SAML rollout for a later date.
- Interim options: we can help with provisioning workflows (SCIM or manual invites) while SAML is being scoped.

Next step — quick call?
Can we schedule a 30-minute call this week to:
- Confirm your IT/security needs,
- Help your team test OAuth2 sign-in,
- Decide whether to prioritize SAML and, if so, capture IdP details and agree on schedule.

Please reply with a preferred time or a contact on your IT team. We’re excited to get this working smoothly for you.

Thanks,
[Your name — PM]
CC: Josh (Sales), Alan (Engineering)

— Internal note for team (not for Acme): Sales previously told Acme SAML would be available “after the auth migration” and Acme expects SAML by end of Q1. Engineering scoped SAML out of this phase and estimated ~3 weeks. Recommend a short alignment call (Sales + Engineering + PM) before we promise a SAML timeline. Huge win — onboarding completion up to 85% (from 60%) — Josh, fantastic work getting this redesigned and shipping so fast.

Quick facts so everyone has the same picture:
- Old flow: 8 steps, 60% completion, median time 12m. Activation = first milestone.
- New flow: 5 steps, 85% completion, median time 4m. Removed: team invite, integration connect, first task, first milestone.
- Data window: <7 days since launch, so 30-day activation hasn't been measured yet.

Two important flags (so we celebrate correctly):
1) Definition changed. We’re now treating “onboarding completion” as activation instead of “first milestone” — that may inflate the short-term completion metric while changing the thing we actually optimize for (longer-term activation/retention).
2) Short data window. Seven days is not enough to know whether this drives real activation/retention over 30 days.

Concrete next steps (I’ll take point unless someone else wants it):
1) Verify the metric (owner: me/Josh) — confirm the exact events/query used to compute the 85% and the denominator. Due: today.
2) Re-add/ensure instrumentation for the old “first milestone” event + retention funnel so we can compare cohorts (owner: engineering/data). Due: end of week.
3) Run a cohort sanity check: compare first-week behavior of new-onboarded users vs pre-change users on downstream signals (first project depth, milestone creation, 7/30-day retention). If 30-day activation falls below previous 35%, we’ll need to add nudges or reintroduce milestone prompts. Due: within 3 business days.
4) If the cohort check looks good, we’ll treat this as a validated lift and push the messaging/support playbooks that depend on higher activation. If not, decide on the quick rollback/experiment surface by next week.

Again — huge win. This is exactly the kind of measurable move we need before the end-of-March ship. Josh — mind pasting the exact query/event you used for the 85% so I can start the verification?

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text:
Short version / decision request
- Target: cut $1,890/mo (30% of $6,300) without touching production ($2,400).
- Proposal: immediately remove two unused staging environments, right-size dev infra, and trim non-critical logging/monitoring. This yields ~$1,910/mo (~30.3%).
- Ask: approval to execute the steps below. Execution can start today and should be done within 48–72 hours with low risk if we take the mitigations listed.

The math (so there’s no ambiguity)
- Current total: $6,300/mo
- 30% target = $6,300 * 0.30 = $1,890/mo savings required
- Proposed savings total = $1,910/mo (slightly over target)

Proposed actions (high-impact, low-risk first)
1) Delete staging-2 ($500/mo) and staging-3 ($500/mo) — save $1,000
   - Why: staging-3 never used; staging-2 was for one-off billing migration testing (last used Feb 10).
   - Risk: we may need these for future migration/testing. Mitigation: take AMI snapshots + DB backups and export any necessary configs before deletion so we can rebuild quickly. Add an explicit “recreate playbook” in the repo.
   - Estimated effort: 1 engineer, 0.5–1 day.

2) Right-size dev database: db.r5.large ($400) -> db.t3.medium ($60) — save $340
   - Why: dev DB is same size as prod but dev usage is low.
   - Risk: developer workflows could be slower; if we need prod-sized dev temporarily, we can restore a snapshot to a larger instance for short runs. Mitigation: schedule migrations to larger instance only when needed and document how.
   - Effort: snapshot + modify instance class; 1 engineer, ~1 day including tests.

3) Right-size dev Redis: cache.r5.large ($300) -> cache.t3.small ($30) — save $270
   - Same rationale/risks as dev DB. Take snapshots/backups and document restore steps.
   - Effort: 1 engineer, ~0.5 day.

4) Reduce dev monitoring agent / plan: $200 -> ~$50 — save $150
   - Why: dev only needs basic metrics; full Datadog agent not required.
   - Risk: less telemetry in dev; mitigation: keep lightweight metrics and enable full agent on-demand.
   - Effort: 0.5 day.

5) Reduce log retention from 90 days to 14 days — saves ~$150 (est)
   - Notes: logging pipeline: ES cluster $800 + retention ~ $400 = $1,200. We keep ES cluster for search but cut retention (team searches last 7 days typically).
   - Risk: investigating incidents spanning >14 days becomes harder. Mitigation: export or archive critical logs before retention window, keep alerting indices or slow-log subsets beyond 14 days if needed.
   - Effort: 0.5 day.

New monthly totals after changes
- Savings: 500 + 500 + 340 + 270 + 150 + 150 = $1,910
- New monthly cost: $6,300 - $1,910 = $4,390 (≈30.3% reduction)

Risks and mitigations (explicit)
- Loss of test capacity: deleting staging environments reduces parallel testing. Mitigation: keep staging-1 as full mirror; create temporary staging-2 on-demand from snapshots if we need it for a migration or heavy testing (rebuild steps documented).
- Developer friction from smaller dev instances: mitigate with quick upscale playbooks and scheduled windows for larger runs.
- Reduced log retention impacting post-incident analysis: mitigate by archiving/ exporting critical logs and keeping important alert indices longer (or storing compressed exports in S3 for long-term needs).
- One-off tasks and migration schedule: if there’s any upcoming migration or billing change that needs staging-2, we must coordinate to recreate it temporarily. Confirm timeline with teams.

Operational checklist to execute safely (what I’ll do / whom to assign)
- Before deletion/downsize:
  - Tag and snapshot AMIs, DB snapshots, Redis backups (Owner: Engineer A) – 2 hours
  - Export staging-2 configs & document rebuild steps (Owner: Engineer A) – 1 hour
- Execute downsizes & retention change in off-hours; validate dev workflows the same day (Owner: Engineer B) – 1 day
- Post-change: confirm billing dashboard and set Slack alert on cost anomalies (Owner: PM) – 1 day
- Add automation: schedule non-prod instances to shut down nights/weekends (follow-up work to reduce spend further) — backlog item.

Follow-ups (30/60/90 day)
- 30 days: confirm actual savings on next invoice and validate no missed impacts.
- 60 days: implement scheduled start/stop for dev/staging to save more (target another 5–10% non-prod).
- 90 days: review ES cluster sizing and long-term logging strategy — consider moving to cheaper log query options or smaller ES cluster if usage low.

If you want a safer, smaller-step alternative
- If you prefer not to delete any staging now, we can instead immediately resize dev DB/Redis and reduce monitoring + retention. That saves ~$910 (340+270+150+150) — about 14.4% total (not enough to meet Finance’s 30% target). So we’ll still miss the ask unless we remove at least one staging or make deeper logging/ES changes.

Recommendation
- Approve the full plan above (remove staging-2 and staging-3, right-size dev DB/Redis, trim dev monitoring, and reduce log retention). It meets Finance’s 30% goal, is reversible, and keeps production untouched. I’ll coordinate snapshots, execute the changes, and report back with invoice delta within 30 days.

Say yes and I’ll schedule the snapshots and deletions now.

EVIDENCE: The response explicitly mentions "staging-3 never used" (line 13) and proposes deleting "staging-3 ($500/mo)" as part of the cost-cutting plan. This directly identifies the load-testing environment that was never actually used.

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: Yes — three tickets look like one root cause tied to migration batch-7 (run 2026-02-25). This is a high-priority incident (revenue + enterprise customer impacted). I recommend we treat it as P0, stop further migrations until fixed, and start a focused investigation + customer comms now.

What I see (facts)
- TICKET-1041 (Pinnacle, Enterprise): invoice shows $0 but they are on Growth plan.
- TICKET-1047 (Redstone, Growth): plan shows Free but they are paying.
- TICKET-1043 (Waverly Labs, Pro): billing page slow / times out (30+s).
- All three were in migration batch-7, run 2026-02-25.
- Other tickets this week are lower severity/resolved or feature requests.

Why this matters
- Multiple paying customers (including an Enterprise) are seeing incorrect billing/plan data or timeouts. That risks revenue, churn, and escalation.
- All three pointing to the same migration batch is strong signal the migration corrupted or partially migrated billing/subscription data or caused performance regressions (e.g., heavy backfill causing DB load).
- With the end-of-March shipping deadline and a two-person team, this can consume critical bandwidth — we should triage now and prevent more impacted customers.

Immediate recommended actions (next 0–2 hours)
1. Declare incident / P0 and owner: assign an engineer to lead investigation and one to own customer comms (support + PM). If we have an on-call rota, page them now.
2. Pause further migration batches immediately until we understand the issue (prevent more customers being affected).
3. Customer communication: send a brief acknowledgement to all affected accounts (use account IDs in tickets) saying we see the problem, we're investigating, and we'll follow up with ETA in X hours. Suggested ETA: initial update in 2 hours, full ETA in 6 hours. (I can draft text if helpful.)
4. Triage checklist for the engineer
   - Pull migration batch-7 run logs and steps. Identify any errors or retries on 2026-02-25.
   - Check the size/count of batch-7 (how many accounts total). Flag how many could be affected.
   - Inspect subscription & billing mapping for affected accounts:
     - Verify subscription_id exists and maps to correct plan_id for acct_pinnacle_882, acct_redstone_447, acct_waverly_091.
     - Inspect invoices table for $0 invoice rows and the invoice-generation job logs for those accounts.
   - Check for cache / index issues causing stale or default “Free” tier in UI (e.g., cache miss falling back to Free).
   - For performance/timeouts (Waverly): check slow queries, DB CPU/IO, long-running migration tasks, queue/backfill workers, and read-replica lag. Correlate timestamps with batch-7 run.
   - Check whether any feature-flags were toggled during migration that could affect billing.
   - If backfill/worker jobs are still running, evaluate abort vs throttle vs re-run strategy.
5. If a quick rollback is possible (db snapshot / revert migration script), prepare a runbook and test it on one account or staging before global action.

Short-term remediation options (depending on root cause)
- If mapping data corrupted: run a deterministic repair script to restore correct plan_id/subscription for affected accounts and re-generate invoices if needed.
- If cache or index was the issue: invalidate cache and rebuild indexes; then re-check UI.
- If migration is causing heavy DB load: pause workers, scale DB (if possible), or move backfill to off-peak window and prioritize hotfix for invoices/plan correctness.

Quantify risk & resources
- Known affected accounts: at least 3 (including an Enterprise). Unknown total affected — we must find batch-7 size ASAP.
- Estimated engineering time (very rough): investigation 2–4 hours; simple repair 2–8 hours; full rollback or complex fixes could be 1–2 days. This could derail other March work — hence pause migrations.

Other tickets (lower priority)
- TICKET-1046 (API docs wrong endpoint) – easy fix, schedule as quick PR; not blocking.
- TICKET-1045 (CSV upload size limit) & TICKET-1044 (dark mode) – backlog items.
- TICKET-1042 (auth) – already resolved.

Suggested messages
- Support -> affected customers (short)
  "Thanks — we see your ticket and we're investigating an issue affecting billing/plan display for some accounts after a recent migration. We're working on a fix now and will update you within 2 hours. Apologies for the disruption — we know this is important and prioritizing it."

Next decision I need from leadership/engineering
- Do we pause further migrations immediately? (Strongly recommended.)
- Who owns incident engineering? Name or on-call so I can assign and notify.
- Do we want me to draft customer-facing status and post to affected accounts and internal Slack incident channel?

If you want, I’ll:
- Draft the support message and an incident Slack post.
- Start the “how many accounts in batch-7” check and surface the list of potentially affected account IDs.

Bottom line: treat this as an incident tied to migration batch-7. Pause migrations, investigate migration logs and billing mappings, communicate to impacted customers, and prepare a repair or rollback plan now.

scenarios.response_text:
Short, decision-focused agenda for Thursday Mar 9 — 2:00pm PST (60 min)

Why this meeting matters (one line)
- Two remaining open questions in Sarah’s API v2 doc are blocking implementation (pagination + webhook payload). Alan flagged that the webhook format must be locked this week or we miss the Mar 15 webhook-format lock — which would jeopardize the end-of-March ship.

- Maria or volunteer: scribe decisions and actions.

- 2:00–2:05 (5m) — Opening & success criteria (PM)
  - State explicit outcomes we need from this meeting:
    1) Pagination strategy decided and owner assigned.
    2) Webhook payload format decided (nested / flat / hybrid) with owners, doc update deadline, and migration plan start date.
  - Remind the Mar 15 webhook-format lock deadline Alan called out in thread.

- 2:05–2:10 (5m) — TL;DR of the doc (Sarah)

  - Confirm decision. If consensus exists (Sarah + Alan already aligned per pre-read), record decision and immediate next actions:

Decision rules (to avoid rehashing)

(If these dates are too aggressive, we must accept shipping risk.)

If yes, I’ll post and assign scribe/timekeeper.

scenarios.response_text:
Welcome — excited you’re joining the billing team Monday (March 10). Below is a focused first‑day orientation: what matters now, where to look, and a short checklist so you can be productive immediately without getting lost in noise.

High‑priority context (read this first)
- Deadline: Billing Migration Phase 2 must ship by March 31. This is the team’s North Star — no extension.
- Critical blocker: Ticket #847 — legacy subscriptions need reconciliation before cutover. Alan is leading this and estimates ~2 more weeks; if reconciliation slips past March 15 we will miss the March 31 target. Treat anything touching reconciliation as high priority and flag delays immediately.
- Dependency you’ll hit: The billing webhook format must be finalized before Sarah can complete API v2 work. Sarah is part‑time (M/W/F), so timing here is fragile — plan syncs accordingly.

Who to go to for what
- Alan — billing lead and owner of reconciliation (ticket #847). Heads down right now; expect slower replies. PST timezone. Dentist appointment Wed (Mar 12) morning — back by noon.
- Josh — product + docs, best person to pair with your first week. PST. Use him for walkthroughs, codebase orientation, and onboarding questions.
- Sarah — auth/webhooks lead. London (GMT), works Monday/Wednesday/Friday only. She owns webhook format and API v2. Don’t schedule anything with her on Tue/Thu.

Immediate first‑day checklist (priority order)
1. Confirm access
   - GitHub repo(s) for billing and migrations
   - CI build status / pipeline access

scenarios.name: scope_surgeon
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: ship a CSV MVP now, not CSV+PDF+Excel. Engineering can deliver a production CSV export in ~5 working days (2 days dev + 3 days QA/docs). Building PDF and Excel as well adds ~10 more working days (total ~15), which delays revenue and gives no customer benefit today — customer research shows all 3 prospects said CSV is sufficient.

Why: numbers and evidence
- Customer research (Josh, Mar 6): Acme, Pinnacle, Waverly all said CSV would meet their needs; no customer asked for PDF; Excel was requested by a rep, not customers.
- Engineering detailed estimate: CSV = 2 days; PDF = 5 days; Excel = 5 days; testing/docs = 3 days. CSV+testing = ~5 working days. All three formats = ~15 working days.
- Sales estimate of 3 deals = ~$120K ARR waiting. If CSV alone closes them, shipping CSV first converts revenue sooner and preserves runway for later work.

Risks if we insist on all three now
- 10 extra working days of engineering before any export ships — risks missing other milestones and delays revenue.
- Opportunity cost: those 10 days block work we could use to iterate based on real customer feedback.
- Possible unnecessary engineering complexity: PDF/Excel add layout, formatting, and edge cases that we may never need if customers adopt CSV.

Recommended plan (what I’ll do if you approve)
1) Ship CSV MVP only.
   - Target: 5 working days from sign-off (2 dev + 3 QA/docs).
   - Acceptance criteria: on-demand CSV export endpoint, streamed download, correct columns (see request below), access controlled to account admins, UTF-8, comma-separated, date in ISO (or customer-preferred format).
2) Post-launch: schedule Excel and PDF as follow-ups if customers explicitly request and commit. Engineering estimates: +10 working days combined.
3) Contingencies:
   - If a customer absolutely insists on Excel/PDF before signing, Sales must escalate with written confirmation that they will not sign without it; we’ll then re-prioritize but we’ll need a PO or signed LOI to justify the extra engineering lead time.
   - Engineering can provide manual one-off CSV exports for urgent closes while the feature ships (if needed).

What I need from Sales now (actions)
1) Confirm approval to ship CSV-only MVP within 5 working days. If you approve, I’ll schedule engineering tomorrow.
2) For each of the three prospects, get written confirmation (email OK) that:
   - CSV is acceptable for closing the deal, and
   - What exact columns/mappings they require (Acme: QuickBooks import mapping — we need column names).
3) Provide any nonstandard requirements per customer:
   - Expected max rows per export (so we can validate performance)
   - Need for scheduled/recurring exports vs on-demand
   - Any compliance/PII masking requirements or retention rules
4) If any customer refuses CSV-only, tell me which one and paste their exact asks so we can decide whether to prioritize Excel/PDF for that single customer.

Suggested short message you can send to the three prospects (optional — I can send it)
- “Quick update: we can deliver an on-demand CSV billing export that includes the columns needed for QuickBooks/imports within ~5 business days. Can you confirm CSV will work for your reconciliation and, if so, send the exact columns/format your team needs? If you require Excel/PDF specifically, please confirm that in writing so we can prioritize it.”

Why this is the right trade-off
- It unlocks revenue fastest (shipping in ~5 days vs ~15).
- It matches what customers told us they need.
- It avoids building features customers didn’t ask for, preserving engineering time for follow-ups or integrations that will actually move the deal.

If you want, I’ll:
- Draft the emails to each prospect (including column checklist),
- Kick off the engineering ticket and sprint plan once Sales confirms, and
- Track sign-offs so we can proceed without surprises.

Tell me: do you approve CSV-only MVP now? If yes, paste one email or the three prospect contacts and I’ll draft/send the exact messages and open the engineering ticket.

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text:
Quick summary and decision recommendation

- Decision (my call to unblock): Use the JWT work we already built, but surface it to customers as a simple bearer token (Authorization: Bearer <token>) with long-ish default lifetime and immediate support for rotation/revocation in the dashboard/API. In short: keep Alan’s developer experience (curl + bearer token) while keeping Sarah’s security capabilities (revocation/rotation, token metadata).
- Why: We keep momentum (two weeks of JWT work is not thrown away), minimize onboarding friction for our target customers (5‑person startups), and reduce our liability by enabling revoke/rotate now rather than leaving keys permanently valid.

Trade-offs (numbers & rationale)
- Speed vs security: Building a fully-fledged short‑lived access token + refresh flow is higher confidence security but costs extra product+engineering time that risks missing the end‑of‑March shipping deadline. We need to prioritize shipping.
- Compromise specifics:
  - Token format: JWT (already implemented).
  - Developer experience: Use bearer token header so curl/quickstart is trivial (one curl example).
  - Default expiry: 30 days. Rationale: balances friction and security for small teams; short enough to limit blast radius, long enough to avoid churn and support tickets. (If someone pushes back, we can consider 90 days, but 30 is a safer starting point.)
  - Rotation/revocation: Must be supported at launch — token revoke endpoint + dashboard "revoke/rotate" button. Implement as a simple revocation list or token version check. This is the minimum to mitigate leaked token liability.

Immediate risks (quantified where possible)
- Liability if a token leaks and we have no revoke: severity high. Probability low if early customers are few, but impact is product/brand and could block sales. Mitigation: implement revocation -> reduces exposure from indefinite to up-to-expiry window (<=30 days).
- Developer confusion: risk of inconsistent docs between “API keys” messaging and technical JWTs. Mitigation: single quickstart doc + copy that says “bearer token (works like a classic API key)”.
- Team capacity: two people only. Adding revocation + docs + tests estimated 3–5 engineering days total (see breakdown below). This fits the deadline if we prioritize.

Concrete next steps (owner + ETA)
1. Decide and lock behavior (this message): PM (me) — now. Deadline: today.
2. Implement token revocation/rotation API and dashboard action: Sarah (auth lead) — 2–3 days.
   - API: POST /tokens/:id/revoke
   - Dashboard: “Revoke / Rotate” button that generates a new token and invalidates the old
   - Revocation implementation: store token jti in DB and check on each request (or versioning per user)
3. Set token lifetime to 30 days and enforce server-side expiry: Alan (backend) — 0.5 days.
4. Quickstart doc and curl examples that show Authorization: Bearer <token> + note about rotation: Me (PM) + Alan — 0.5–1 day.
5. QA & Add two regression tests: token expiry enforcement and revocation path — Alan & Sarah — 0.5–1 day.
6. Communicate to customers/internal: single-sentence public doc change + changelog — PM — when merged.

Total estimated effort: 3–5 engineering days. This keeps us on track for end‑of‑March.

If anyone disagrees
- If Sarah insists we must ship only short‑lived tokens + refresh flow now, show the concrete ETA/plan and trade-offs (extra ~1–2 sprints of work, higher risk to missing March deadline). We should escalate only if security/legal require it now.
- If Alan insists on plain API keys with no revocation, point out liability and marketing risk; ask for a concrete mitigation plan (e.g., require immediate dashboard rotation on onboarding, insurance).

Call to action (what I need from both of you now)
- Alan: confirm you can add server-side expiry and help with the quickstart examples.
- Sarah: confirm you can implement the revocation endpoint + dashboard flow in the timeframe above.
- Both: reply with "ack" or an exception within 6 hours. If no objections, I will file the implementation tickets, assign owners, and update the launch checklist.

Closing note
This is a pragmatic compromise that minimizes risk to shipping while giving us the security controls Sarah rightly wants. It keeps the UX Alan is advocating for and preserves the two weeks of work already done. If we get paying customers, we will prioritize migrating to short‑lived access tokens + refresh flow in the next sprint with a clear migration plan.

Your move: ack or raise a concrete blocker (no vague objections) within 6 hours.

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: No‑go for a full rollout on Friday in the current state. We have two high‑impact, unresolved risks that make a full launch unsafe: (1) the load test is not completed, and (2) there is no delivery confirmation or customer-facing delivery log. Both can cause silent, customer‑visible billing failures that we can’t detect or recover from quickly.

Why I’m saying No (concrete risks)
- Missing load test (pre‑launch checklist item unchecked). Without a load test that simulates production traffic patterns we don’t know whether throughput, retries, DLQ behavior, or backpressure will hold under real load. A production overload can cause widespread delays/duplicates or DLQ saturation. That’s a system‑level failure that will impact all customers.
- No delivery confirmation mechanism + no customer delivery log. If we send a webhook and the customer endpoint returns 200 but doesn’t actually process it, we have no way to detect or prove delivery. That’s a direct path to billing disputes and lost revenue. The current system offers only dead letter queue for permanent failures — that doesn’t cover the “returned 200 but not processed” failure mode.
- Rollback plan is plausible (15 minutes) but incomplete in practice. Point‑in‑time DB recovery and feature-flagging API routing are good, but they don’t address:
  - How we detect and reconcile events already sent to customers before rollback (risk of double‑bill or missing records).
  - The time and manual effort to reconcile customer statements after rollback. The 15‑minute number is optimistic unless reconciliation tooling is verified.
- Monitoring + on‑call gaps to pressure‑test: dashboards are updated, on‑call rotation is confirmed — but we don’t have evidence the alerts/runs have been tested end‑to‑end, or that runbooks exist for the exact failure modes above.
- Customer support impact: without a delivery log or replay tooling, support will be forced into slow manual reconciliations. That creates churn and damages trust on day one.

What “go” looks like (must be completed before a full rollout)
1. Load test with production traffic patterns
   - Run the scheduled load test, capture results, and fix any bottlenecks. Acceptance: no sustained increase in 5xx or retry rates above agreed thresholds, DLQ growth acceptable, and latency within SLA under target load.
   - Owner: Eng. Deadline: complete and verified at least 48 hours before launch (i.e., by Wednesday EOD if launch is Friday).
2. Delivery visibility + replay capability
   - Minimum viable requirement: internal queryable delivery log per customer that shows event id, timestamps, HTTP response codes, and retry attempts; ability for ops to replay events from the DLQ per customer.
   - Preferable: expose a customer-facing delivery history (or an interim support view we can share).
   - Owner: Eng + Ops. Target: implement minimal internal log and replay by Wednesday EOD.
3. Failure simulation and rollback + reconciliation test
   - Run failure scenarios including: customer endpoint returning 200 but not processing, endpoint flapping, DLQ explosion, and full rollback. Validate reconciliation steps and measure time to recover and reconcile.
   - Acceptance: documented runbook that proves we can detect the 200‑but‑unprocessed case (or at least detect downstream discrepancies), plus a tested reconciliation flow.
   - Owner: Eng + SRE. Deadline: Wednesday EOD.
4. Monitoring & runbooks
   - Alerts for silent failures (e.g., high rate of acked webhooks but no downstream downstream processing metric, sudden divergence between sent webhooks and billing records).
   - On‑call runbook with owner, escalation path, and playbook for customer escalations.
   - Owner: SRE/Oncall. Deadline: Wednesday EOD.
5. Customer communications + opt‑in pilot
   - Update customer communications to explicitly state the current limitations and recommended idempotent processing on their side.
   - Strongly prefer a phased launch: a small pilot (1–5 customers) for the first 48–72 hours to limit blast radius while we validate.
   - Owner: PM (you) + Customer Success. Ready before pilot start.

If those items are completed and tested, we can greenlight a limited pilot on Friday. Only after pilot metrics look good (48–72 hours) should we expand to full rollout.

If we can’t complete this by Wednesday EOD (48 hours before Friday):
- Don’t do a full rollout. The risk of silent failures and large customer impact is too high.
- Options instead of full rollback:
  - Run a pilot with a controlled subset of customers (recommended).
  - Or postpone full rollout by 1 week to finish the items above.

Immediate next actions I recommend for the next 24 hours (who & what)
1. Eng: Run the scheduled load test now. Share results and any mitigations needed (capacity, retry tuning). (Owner: Eng lead — start now)
2. Eng: Implement minimal internal delivery log + replay API for DLQ. If full customer‑facing UI is too large, at least make logs exportable for support. (Owner: Eng)
3. SRE/Oncall: Draft and test runbooks for the 3 failure modes above; set alert thresholds and verify pages. (Owner: SRE)
4. PM: Update customer communications and prepare pilot invitation email + in‑app banner for pilot customers. (Owner: PM/CS)
5. Decision checkpoint Wednesday EOD: if load test + log/replay + runbook + pilot plan are green → allow pilot Friday; else postpone.

Why a pilot is the pragmatic alternative
- Limits exposure and gives time to detect and fix the “returned 200 but not processed” problem in the wild.
- Lowers customer support load while we iterate on visibility and reconciliation tools.
- Still keeps momentum toward the end‑of‑March deadline.

Bottom line
- Full production rollout on Friday: No, not yet.
- Pilot rollout (1–5 customers) on Friday: Yes, only if load test, internal delivery logs + replay, and runbook tests complete by Wednesday EOD and on‑call is staffed.

I’ll own coordinating the Wednesday checkpoint: gather the load test results, delivery log readiness, and runbook test outcomes and give a definitive go/no‑go for pilot vs. full rollout. Tell me who on Eng and SRE will execute the items above and I’ll schedule the checkpoint.

scenarios.name: timezone_play
scenarios.response_text:
Short version — we break the logjam with a tight async decision + customer input window, a clear decision doc, and a fallback that I (PM) will decide if stakeholders don’t converge by the cutoff. That satisfies Sarah’s request for simplicity for consumers while giving Alan a way to include structured data without forcing nested parsing for every webhook.

Why: we need a decision by March 12 (per memory). The thread has stalled 5 days. A missing decision blocks Alan’s webhook work and Sarah’s API v2 contract work. We can’t wait for a synchronous meeting — there are zero overlapping hours for all three.

Concrete plan (what I’ll do unless you tell me otherwise)

1) I’ll produce a 1-page Decision Doc (PM-owned, < 200 words plus two example payloads) and send it to Alan, Sarah, and Takeshi within the next business hour.
   - Contents: short problem statement, two concrete options (Flat-only vs. Flat + optional nested "details"), recommended option (Flat + optional nested), example JSON for both, concise trade-offs, and the specific questions we need answered.

2) Ask for constrained async responses only

5) If disagreement persists at cutoff → I will make the call and document rationale.

   - I’ll also include a one-week backwards-compatibility plan (e.g., optional field introduced, consumers opt-in, server validates both formats for one release) so Sarah/Sara’s v2 timeline isn’t surprised.

scenarios.name: debt_ledger
scenarios.response_text:
I get why you want to keep the team laser-focused on customer-facing work. With 25 days until the deadline, every hour should pull us toward first revenue.

That said — ignoring these specific tech-debt items is a measured gamble that, given the evidence, I don’t think we can afford. Here’s why, with concrete costs and a low-effort plan to fix the biggest risks.

Why we should prioritize tech debt (evidence + risk)
- Hardcoded billing URL (seen repeatedly: Feb 12, Feb 19, Mar 3, Mar 5). Cost: ~1 hour/week wasted tracking/changing it; failure modes include billing pointing at prod/staging mixups (we already saw tests fail because of this on Mar 3). Risk: a billing mistake before launch is high-impact (customer charges, legal/regulatory, customer trust).
- Retry logic without backoff (Feb 8, Feb 22, Mar 1). Symptoms: duplicate webhooks, customer complaints, endpoint hammering. Risk: causes customer-facing duplicates/outages and creates support tickets that will steal dev time during the launch window.
- Generic error messages (Feb 10, Feb 24, Mar 6). Cost: ~1 hour/week diagnosing issues. Risk: every minute we spend chasing “something went wrong” is time not building features or responding to new customer issues.
- Skipped webhook test (skipped since Jan 20; Feb 5 noted it would have caught BUG-204). Risk: skipped test has already let a production bug through. That’s a clear direct path to a launch-blocking incident.

Numbers that matter
- Current recurring cost (workspace estimates): ~2.75 hours/week.
- Remaining time to deadline: ~3.5–4 weeks → recurring waste ≈ 10–11 hours total if we do nothing.
- Estimated effort to buy down these risks (realistic, focused fixes): ~12–16 hours total (1.5–2 person-days). That’s roughly equal to the wasted time over the same period — and removes outsized risk that could blow the whole launch.

Low-risk, high-impact plan (what I recommend we do this sprint)
1. Immediate triage (15 minutes): agree owners and lock scope — don’t let this turn into “refactor everything.”
2. Must-fix items (total estimate ~12–16 hrs):
   - Fix hardcoded billing URL/config (3–4 hrs) — single config + one PR; run the billing tests.
   - Add retry backoff to webhook logic and cap retries (4–6 hrs) — simple exponential/backoff + idempotency guard where possible.
   - Re-enable the webhook integration test and fix flakiness (3–4 hrs) — catching BUG-204-style regressions is high leverage.

scenarios.response_text:
Short answer / recommendation
- Do a 4-hour engineering spike now (Sarah + 1). If the spike shows the change is <=2 full dev-days, implement Ed25519 before our ship. If it’s >2 days, defer implementation but immediately give Sales a clear statement + timeline and an interim workaround.
- Rationale: Sales already has at least two prospects asking (Acme, Pinnacle). Sarah’s note says the signature logic is modular — so this is likely a small, localized change and high ROI. But we cannot let this distract from the end-of-March ship if it’s bigger than expected.

Why this path
- Upside if small: We remove a clear competitive objection and avoid losing evaluation momentum with prospects. The competitive changelog shows Rival already shipped this — customers will ask more. Sarah’s code modularity means low technical risk.
- Downside if big: We’re two people with a hard ship deadline (end of March). Adding a multi-day task now could push other committed work and jeopardize first revenue. We must measure effort before committing.

Concrete risks to flag (and mitigation)
1. Effort mis-estimate
   - Risk: It sounds small, but key management, SDK updates, tests, docs, and security review can expand scope.
   - Mitigation: The spike must list all downstream work (private key storage, rotation, API headers, SDKs, docs, QA). Only commit if total is <=2 dev-days extra.

2. Key management & security
   - Risk: Ed25519 uses asymmetric keys — we must securely store the private signing key and provide public key distribution. That may require HSM/secret-storage work or a security review.
   - Mitigation: Spike must confirm where private keys live (secrets manager vs. new infra) and capture required approvals.

3. Backwards compatibility and rollout
   - Risk: Customers expect both legacy HMAC and new Ed25519 during migration.
   - Mitigation: Implement support for both algorithms concurrently and include an algorithm header (e.g., X-Signature-Algorithm) so customers can detect which was used.

4. Sales/expectation management
   - Risk: If we say yes and miss delivery, credibility hurt.
   - Mitigation: If we can’t commit to immediate implementation, provide Sales with an honest, dated plan and an interim workaround.

Spike deliverables (4 hours)
- PoC: sign one webhook using Ed25519 and verify it end-to-end.
- List of required changes and rough estimates for each: signing code, private key storage/rotation, header changes, SDKs, docs, QA, security sign-off.
- Decision recommendation: implement now vs. defer.

Implementation acceptance criteria (if we proceed)
- End-to-end signing + verification tests pass.
- Private key stored securely (secrets manager or equivalent) and rotation plan documented.
- API/header change to indicate algorithm added; support both algorithms.
- Docs + sample code updated.
- Release notes and Sales script ready.

If spike shows small effort: target timeline
- Spike: today (4 hours)
- Dev + tests + docs + QA: 1–2 working days
- Release: within this week (assuming no infra/security blockers)

If spike shows larger effort: messaging for Sales (use this verbatim)

  “We’ve prioritized Ed25519 support and are actively assessing implementation. We expect to ship an official Ed25519-signed webhook option by [DATE]. In the meantime, we continue to support HMAC-SHA256 (industry standard) and can provide a technical migration plan and sample verifier code for your security team. If Ed25519 is a hard blocker for your evaluation, tell us and we’ll prioritize the work for your account.”
- Action: Sales to reply with who needs this and how firm the requirement is (soft preference vs. deal-breaker).

Other product considerations (don’t lose sight)

2. Ask Sales (Josh) to list which prospects want Ed25519 and whether it’s a hard requirement.
3. Reconvene with spike results in <24 hours and decide: implement immediately or prepare Sales messaging + roadmap date.

If you want, I’ll draft the Sales message and open the spike ticket with acceptance criteria and test cases — tell me who to assign and I’ll create it now.

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
Executive summary — recommendation
- Do NOT run the full 3-week payment-service refactor now if the objective is to ship by end-of-March. The refactor (15 working days) will push us past the hard deadline unless we accept missing it. Instead: ship an MVP billing surface for first paying customers now (no full refactor), then run the 3-week refactor immediately after we hit revenue — or run a much smaller targeted change now (see options). If leadership is willing to fund an external contractor, we can do the refactor in parallel without blocking shipping.

Key numbers (from the workspace)
- Current time to add a billing feature: 5 days.
- Post-refactor time to add a billing feature: ~2 days.
- Refactor estimated effort: 3 weeks (Alan full-time = ~15 working days).
- Planned Q2 billing features: 6 features.
- Break-even of full refactor: at ~5 features (workspace math).
- Time left to deadline: ~25 days (end of March).
- CEO position: “We cannot afford 3 weeks without shipping.”

Concrete scenario math
- A. No refactor, build 6 features: 6 × 5 = 30 days → misses deadline by ~5 days.
- B. Full refactor then 6 features: 15 + (6 × 2) = 27 days → misses deadline by ~2 days.
- C. Ship MVP (3 highest-value billing features) without refactor: 3 × 5 = 15 days → meets deadline with runway left.
- D. Full refactor before a 3-feature MVP: 15 + (3 × 2) = 21 days → would also meet deadline but delays any shipping by 15 days and concentrates all risk on Alan.
- E. Targeted, small refactor (example: extract Stripe adapter + shared test fixtures) estimated 5–7 days + reduces per-feature time to ~3 days: 5 + (6 × 3) = 23 days → meets deadline and gives partial long-term benefit. (This is an estimate — assumptions below.)

How to interpret these numbers
- The full refactor only becomes clearly positive if we will implement ≥5 billing features before we count payback. The workspace projects 6 planned Q2 billing features, so long-term it’s worth doing — but the timing matters relative to the immutable end-of-March shipping target.
- The team is two people and Alan is the single named owner for the refactor. Redirecting him full-time for 3 weeks creates a single-point-of-failure risk for shipping and feature delivery.

Risks if we run the full refactor now
- Missing the hard deadline (CEOs comment indicates no extension).
- Single-engineer bottleneck: Alan unavailable for critical bug fixes or last-minute shipping tasks.
- Refactor introduces regression risk; we’ll need QA cycles and potential rollbacks — more schedule uncertainty.
- Opportunity cost: delaying first revenue and feedback; losing credibility with customers/investors.

Risks if we defer refactor
- Short-term: slower iteration (5 days per feature), higher engineering friction and possibly more review cycles and QA burden.
- Medium-term: continued technical debt may create higher cost per feature, more bugs, slower sales responses.

Practical alternatives (ranked)
1) Ship MVP now; run full refactor after first revenue (recommended for meeting deadline)
   - Deliver the 3 most valuable billing features (estimate: 15 days).
   - Immediately schedule the 3-week refactor to start April 1 (Alan full-time), to unlock faster iteration for Q2.
   - Reason: preserves hard deadline, gets customers and revenue in the door, and reduces risk of losing window.

2) Do a targeted surgical refactor now (if we want some tech progress but cannot spare 15 days)
   - Scope: extract Stripe adapter, unify a small set of validation layers, and add shared test fixtures.
   - Estimated cost: 5–7 days (Alan full-time). Assumption: we only touch the highest-friction files; avoid full consolidation.
   - Expected outcome: reduce feature work from 5 → ~3 days each (not down to 2). With this, planned 6 features become feasible within ~23 days.
   - Risk: estimates are rough; requires careful scoping and aggressive stop-criteria.

3) Hire a short-term contractor/consultant to do the full refactor in parallel
   - Contractor works full-time on the refactor for 3 weeks while Alan finishes product shipping.
   - Trade-off: incremental cost vs. preserving deadline and getting the long-term benefits sooner.
   - Ask finance for budget estimate and expedited onboarding plan. Also plan code review and pair sessions so knowledge doesn’t become siloed.

Implementation plan I recommend now (practical, low risk)
1) Immediate decision (today):
   - Approve shipping the MVP billing surface (top 3 billing features). Owner: Product/Alan. Target: ship by end-of-March.
   - If leadership insists on refactor now, choose one option: (a) fund contractor or (b) accept being ~2–5 days late.

2) Short-term (this week):
   - Product triage: identify top 3 billing features that unlock paying customers. Document acceptance criteria. Owner: Product.
   - Engineering: freeze all nonessential refactor work. Allocate Alan to MVP tasks. If we pursue targeted refactor, scope it and timebox to 5 days.
   - QA: plan a minimal test matrix for the MVP; reserve time for regressions.

3) Post-MVP (immediate after revenue or on April 1):
   - Start full 3-week refactor (Alan full-time) OR execute contractor plan if approved.
   - Deliverables: single consolidated billing module, separated Stripe adapter, shared test fixtures, and CI checks to reduce mocking.
   - Measure: confirm per-feature time reduces toward 2 days within 2 sprints.

What I will do as PM
- If you approve the MVP-first approach, I will:
  - Run a 24–48 hour triage with Alan to pick the exact 3 features and confirm the 15‑day implementation plan.
  - Draft the short refactor scope for a targeted 5-day “surgical” work package we can optionally run in parallel (with clear stop criteria).
  - Prepare a cost estimate and hiring plan for a contractor if leadership wants a parallel full refactor.

Assumptions and confidence
- Confidence in the current numbers from workspace is high (they’re the team’s estimates). The partial-refactor time and its effect on per-feature time are estimates and need engineering sign-off.
- We do not currently have budget or a contractor lined up; hiring would take lead time.

Bottom line
- If “ship by end-of-March” is non-negotiable, do not run the full 3-week refactor before shipping. Ship the MVP billing features now and run the full refactor immediately after. If leadership prefers to invest to reduce future friction without delaying revenue, fund an external contractor to perform the refactor in parallel — I can produce cost and onboarding estimates right away. Which path do you want me to action?

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text:
Title: Postmortem — March 6 billing migration dry-run corruption

Summary
- What happened: On March 6, a billing migration dry-run executed against a production data snapshot corrupted 200 customer subscription records by writing nulls into required columns (incorrect plan tiers and billing amounts).
- Impact: No customer-facing impact (dry-run used a snapshot). 200 legacy subscription records in the snapshot were corrupted. It took ~4 hours to identify and restore the snapshot from backup. If this had been run against live production, the impact would have been customer bill errors and outage risk.
- Key wins: Monitoring alerted in 12 minutes (threshold 15m). Alan’s rollback script restored data successfully. Pre-written customer communications were ready and sent within 30 minutes of detection.
- Root cause: The migration script assumed all subscriptions have a plan_change_date field. ~200 legacy subscriptions (pre-2025) do not have that field; the script wrote nulls into required columns, causing cascading integrity issues.

Timeline (compressed)
- 09:00 — Alan started dry-run against production snapshot.
- 09:12 — Sarah’s monitoring alert fired (data anomalies).
- 09:15 — Alan confirmed corruption, stopped script.
- ~09:30 — Josh sent external comms (no customers impacted).
- 13:00 — All records restored from backup; root cause identified.
- Total time to full restoration and containment: ~4 hours.

What went well
- Monitoring worked: alert fired in ~12 minutes (within our 15-minute threshold).
- Rollback tooling existed and worked; restoration completed within ~4 hours.
- Communication templates were available and used quickly.

What went wrong
- Migration assumptions: script assumed plan_change_date always exists; no defensive handling for missing legacy fields.
- Testing gap: migration was not tested against full production-scale historical data that includes legacy accounts missing expected fields.
- Environment risk: a write-running dry-run against a production snapshot exposed us to high-risk writes; the snapshot was not isolated sufficiently for destructive tests.
- Review/process gap: no mandatory peer review / checklist for running high-risk migrations against production-like data.
- Containment time: 4 hours to identify all affected records and restore is longer than we should tolerate.

Root cause analysis (5 Whys, condensed)
1. Why did records get corrupted? Because the migration wrote nulls into required columns.
2. Why did the migration write nulls? Because plan_change_date was assumed present and nulls propagated into derived fields.
3. Why was plan_change_date missing? Because ~200 legacy subscriptions (pre-2025) don’t have that field.
4. Why wasn’t the script safe for legacy data? Because the script lacked defensive checks and migration tests for legacy cases.
5. Why were those cases not tested? Because we did not run the dry-run against production-scale historical data with legacy patterns, and there was no pre-run validation checklist or peer review.

Immediate mitigations (what we did right away)
- Stopped the dry-run immediately when alerted.
- Rolled back the snapshot using Alan’s rollback script; verified restoration.
- Notified stakeholders and used the pre-written communication template.
- Marked migration as paused until fixes are implemented and validated.

Action items (owners, measurable success criteria, and deadlines)
1) Add defensive checks to migration script (owner: Alan)
- What: Validate presence/format of plan_change_date and explicitly handle missing values (backfill with safe default or skip with audit).
- Success: New script fails fast on missing required fields with clear error logs; unit tests cover legacy accounts.
- Due: March 13, 2026.

2) Add unit + integration tests for migration (owner: Alan)
- What: Add unit tests covering legacy subscription shapes and an integration test that runs migration against a masked production-like sample (includes legacy cases).
- Success: CI green for migration tests; integration run verifies no null writes.
- Due: March 13, 2026.

3) Require peer review & checklist for all migration scripts (owner: PM / me)
- What: Create a mandatory “migration runbook checklist” including preflight validation, targeted test dataset, approval gate.
- Success: Checklist added to repo; PRs for migrations must include completed checklist and at least one reviewer approval.
- Due: March 10, 2026.

4) Change dry-run practice (owner: PM + Engineering)
- What: Prohibit running destructive-write migration code against production snapshots except in an isolated, read-only sandbox or masked copy. For write tests, use a fully isolated staging environment that mirrors production schema but not live identifiers.
- Success: New policy documented and enforced; no further write-dry-runs on production snapshots without explicit Exec sign-off.
- Due: March 10, 2026.

5) Audit other migration scripts for similar assumptions (owner: Alan + Sarah)
- What: Run a sweep of all migration scripts to find assumptions about missing/null fields; add tests or fixes where needed.
- Success: Audit completed, list of fixes and resolutions tracked in backlog.
- Due: March 17, 2026.

6) Improve monitoring and reduce detection time (owner: Sarah)
- What: Add a preflight anomaly detector for dry-run outputs (schema diffs, unexpected nulls, distribution changes) and reduce alert threshold for critical data anomalies from 15m to 5m for migration contexts.
- Success: New detectors firing in test runs; documented SLA for alert response <10 minutes.
- Due: March 13, 2026.

7) Run a verified dry-run with updated controls (owner: Alan)
- What: After fixes, run another dry-run against the isolated/masked snapshot, confirm no corruption, and publish results.
- Success: Dry-run completes with zero corrupted records; results reviewed and signed off.
- Due: March 18, 2026.

8) Update runbooks & communications (owner: Josh)
- What: Update incident and customer-communication runbooks to reflect this incident’s timeline, decision triggers, and contacts.
- Success: Runbooks updated; runbook drill scheduled.
- Due: March 13, 2026.

9) Post-incident review & training (owner: PM)
- What: Run a 60–90 minute retrospective with Alan, Sarah, Josh to confirm learnings and close action items; include a short training for engineers on migration best practices.
- Success: Retro held; action items assigned and tracked.
- Due: March 9, 2026.

Verification criteria (how we’ll know the problem is fixed)
- New migration code has defensive checks and unit/integration tests that include legacy cases; CI passes.
- Policy change prevents destructive dry-runs on production snapshots without isolation.
- A clean dry-run (with new checks and isolated snapshot) completes and is signed off.
- Monitoring detects any subsequent anomalies faster (target: alert within 5–12 minutes; response within 10 minutes).
- Audit of migration scripts completed and remediated.

Risks and follow-ups
- Risk: We may have other migration scripts with similar assumptions. Mitigation: the migration audit (action 5) must be prioritized.
- Risk: If training and policy enforcement are not followed, people may continue ad-hoc dry-runs. Mitigation: require explicit approval and checklist enforcement in PRs.
- Risk: Restores took ~4 hours; if we face a real production corruption, 4 hours may be too long. Mitigation: measure and optimize restore time (runbook optimization, faster snapshot restores) — add as follow-up if not covered above.

Lessons learned (high level)
- Defensive programming matters: migrations must assume legacy/malformed data and fail safely.
- Test with representative data: full historical patterns (not just current schema) must be part of migration testing.
- Don’t run destructive writes against production snapshots without isolation and approvals.
- Monitoring + rollback scripts are effective — invest in both. We were lucky this was a dry-run; the safeguards saved us.
- Process changes (checklists, peer reviews, CI tests) are low-cost ways to reduce high-impact risk.

Communications
- Josh sent the initial comms; we should send an internal summary of the postmortem and action items to Execs and Eng by March 9, 2026. External messaging is unnecessary because customers were not affected, but we will keep messaging ready in case of future incidents.

Closing
This was a near miss: the incident happened in a dry-run, but the underlying gaps would have led to customer-impacting errors if this had been run in production. The remediation items above are prioritized to fix the most critical gaps first (script defensiveness, test coverage, dry-run policy, and mandatory checklists). I will (PM) track all action items in our backlog, follow up daily until the high-priority items are closed, and run the retrospective with Alan, Sarah, and Josh on or before March 9.

If you want, I’ll:
- Create the checklist PR and assign reviewers,
- Block migration runs on production snapshots until we sign off on the new process,
- Schedule the retro and the verification dry-run.

Which of those should I prioritize first?

It also explicitly mentions a "Review/process gap" and does not assign individual blame.

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: I will not sign off. The current plan leaves us dangerously close to Stripe’s 100 req/sec limit with no buffer and no mitigation for spikes — that’s a direct path to 429s on day one of shipping.

Why not (numbers from the workspace)
- Stripe rate limit: 100 req/sec.
- Billing (Alan) planned peak: 60 req/sec.
- API v2 (Sarah) planned peak: 35 req/sec.
- Combined normal peak = 95 req/sec → 95% of limit (only 5 req/sec headroom).
- Typical traffic spikes = 1.5–2× → combined 143–190 req/sec → guaranteed 429 errors.
- No shared client/caching — two independent SDKs/connection pools, so nothing coordinates or smooths bursts.

Risk
- Customer-facing failures (429s) during normal spikes — high severity given we need to convert first customers.
- Intermittent 429s likely to cause retries that amplify load and further worsen outages.
- No plan to increase rate limit and no central throttling — single point of failure at Stripe limit.

Immediate recommended plan (must happen before we ship next week)

1) Stop and fix: do not ship both features concurrently without mitigation.
   - Owner: PM (me) — coordinate. Deadline: immediate.

2) Short-term (24–72 hours) — low-effort changes we should implement now
   - Alan (Billing): stop polling where possible; switch to Stripe webhooks for payment confirmation. Webhooks eliminate the polling load during invoice window. If full webhook migration isn’t possible immediately, reduce polling frequency and add a cache so the processor only hits Stripe for strictly necessary reconciliations.
     - Target: reduce billing peak from 60 -> ≤30 req/sec.
     - Deadline: 48–72 hours.
   - Sarah (API v2): add a short-lived cache for subscription lookups (e.g., 15–60s TTL depending on business tolerance) and local DB-sync for plan changes. Implement per-process rate limiting and jittered retries.
     - Target: reduce API v2 peak from 35 -> ≤15 req/sec.
     - Deadline: 48 hours.
   - Outcome: target combined peak ≤45–55 req/sec (well under 100 with breathing room).

3) Short-to-medium term (this week) — global coordination
   - Implement a simple centralized rate-limiter proxy or shared Stripe client that enforces a global token bucket across both services (or a lightweight API gateway rule that caps aggregate outgoing Stripe requests).
     - Owner: infra/engineering.
     - Deadline: before both ships (this week). If a full proxy isn’t possible, enforce coordinated per-team caps enforced in config and tested.
   - Add robust retry/backoff with jitter and circuit-breaker logic so retries don’t amplify load on 429s.
     - Owner: Sarah/Alan.
     - Deadline: before ship.

4) Parallel: contact Stripe to request an urgent rate-limit increase.
   - Owner: PM / billing lead to open support ticket + account rep outreach.
   - Deadline: today. Acceptance only if Stripe confirms increased limit (target >=200 req/sec) and timeline is before ship.

5) Test and verify
   - Run load tests that simulate normal and 2× spikes against Stripe-limited environment or a mock that returns 429s to validate behavior and client backoff.
   - Owner: QA/Engineering.
   - Deadline: before ship.

Acceptance criteria for sign-off
- EITHER Stripe has committed to an increased rate limit that covers 2× spike headroom (e.g., >=200 req/sec) and we’ve validated it,
- OR combined effective peak after caching/rework/global throttling is <=70 req/sec with simulated 2× spike tests showing graceful degradation (no cascading retries) and no customer-facing 429s in the test window.

If neither is true, we should delay one of the ships (not both) so we don’t put customers at risk.

Immediate asks for Alan and Sarah
- Alan: confirm whether billing can move to webhooks; if not, exactly how you’ll reduce polling (frequency) and by how much. Target reduction numbers and ETA.
- Sarah: confirm caching TTL you can tolerate for subscription lookups and whether you can implement local sync. Provide estimated new req/sec after caching and ETA.
- Infra/Eng: confirm whether a quick shared rate limiter or gateway rule can be implemented this week.

I’ll coordinate the Stripe support request and schedule a 20-minute sync with Alan/Sarah/infra in the next 24 hours to lock decisions. We have one week before both ship — this is urgent.
